coredata.dc:title: Highly active hydrogen production from hydrolysis of potassium borohydride by caffeine carbon quantum dot-supported cobalt catalyst in ethanol solvent by hydrothermal treatment

coredata.dc:description: Herein, the efficiency of the Co catalyst synthesized in caffeine-based carbon quantum dot-supported material using ethanol solvent was evaluated for H2 production from the hydrolysis of KBH4. The chemical compositions and morphology structures of these caffeine carbon dot-supported Co catalysts were carefully characterized by XRD, TEM, BET, EDS, FTIR, XPS, and ICP/OES analyses. XPS, FTIR and EDX analyses showed abundant oxygen atoms on the caffeine carbon dot-supported Co catalyst. It can be attributed to the prevention of aggregation of particles by reducing the average particle size with both caffeine and carbon quantum dots materials. The catalysts prepared in methanol and ethanol solvent by hydrothermal treatment compared to water have significant effects on H2 production from KBH4 hydrolysis. The H2 production rate obtained by Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol) at 30 °C was 17,081 ml min−1g−1. At the same time, the TOF value obtained with 4% KBH4 for the Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol) was found as 2765 h−1. The reusability of Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) for the catalytic hydrolysis of KBH4 for H2 production was evaluated by six consecutive experiments.

head.ce:title: Highly active hydrogen production from hydrolysis of potassium borohydride by caffeine carbon quantum dot-supported cobalt catalyst in ethanol solvent by hydrothermal treatment

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, the efficiency of the Co catalyst synthesized in caffeine-based carbon quantum dot-supported material using ethanol solvent was evaluated for H2 production from the hydrolysis of KBH4. The chemical compositions and morphology structures of these caffeine carbon dot-supported Co catalysts were carefully characterized by XRD, TEM, BET, EDS, FTIR, XPS, and ICP/OES analyses. XPS, FTIR and EDX analyses showed abundant oxygen atoms on the caffeine carbon dot-supported Co catalyst. It can be attributed to the prevention of aggregation of particles by reducing the average particle size with both caffeine and carbon quantum dots materials. The catalysts prepared in methanol and ethanol solvent by hydrothermal treatment compared to water have significant effects on H2 production from KBH4 hydrolysis. The H2 production rate obtained by Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol) at 30 °C was 17,081 ml min−1g−1. At the same time, the TOF value obtained with 4% KBH4 for the Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol) was found as 2765 h−1. The reusability of Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) for the catalytic hydrolysis of KBH4 for H2 production was evaluated by six consecutive experiments.

ce:section.ce:para: Increasing global energy demand and the use of new low-carbon energy based on renewable sources is important to combat serious environmental problems caused by fossil fuels [1]. To contribute to the future carbon-free energy economy, the clean energy carrier hydrogen (H2) offers significant advantages such as a high energy density per unit mass and the release of a harmless substance as a by-product [2]. Fossil fuels, solar energy, green algae, and hydride compounds are used to produce hydrogen. Chemical hydride compounds are seen as important hydrogen production sources with important properties such as high hydrogen storage potential and high amount of hydrogen released [3]. Alkali metals such as Li, Na and K have an excellent capacity to develop borohydride [4]. NaBH4 is an important compound with a hydrogen storage capacity of about 10.8% by weight and high-purity hydrogen release [5–14].

It has a high enthalpy for hydrogen production and significant activation energy [15]. To overcome these negative properties of NaBH4, researchers were interested in different borohydride compounds. KBH4 is a borohydride compound with a melting point of 500 °C, high gravimetric hydrogen density and good hydrolytic properties [16,17]. However, studies on hydrogen production from KBH4 hydrolysis are limited in the literature, probably because KBH4 has lower gravimetric hydrogen concentration and higher production cost than NaBH4 [18,19]. However, compared to NaBH4, KBH4 is more hygroscopic and produces less heat during the hydrolysis process [20].

The hydrolysis of KBH4 with water is indicated by the following reaction [21].
(1)
K B H
4
+ 2
H 2
O
→
4
H 2
+
K B O
2

Heterogeneous and homogeneous catalysts are used to accelerate the hydrolysis reactions of NaBH4 and KBH4 [22–25]. Metal-based catalysts are more attractive in these systems for H2 production. In both NaBH4 and KBH4 hydrolysis, transition metals show significant catalytic activity [26–28].

Although Co and Ni-based catalysts show important catalytic performances for the hydrolysis of borohydride compounds, there are disadvantages such as agglomeration and difficult separation of fine particles due to the exothermic nature of such reactions. Such agglomerations reduce the surface area of the catalyst, limiting catalytic activity. It is one of the solutions envisaged to deposit the relevant metal catalyst on a support material with a high specific surface area to avoid this particle aggregation [29,30].

Support materials are widely used as an effective way to eliminate this negativity, that is, to prevent metal aggregation and to improve the metal dispersion to nanometer size. It has been reported that it is also important to confine metal nanoparticles to the mesoporous support channel to strengthen the metal-support interaction and prevent the aggregation of metal particles [9,31].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs) have received increasing attention as new carbon materials with great application potential. Because of their nanometer size, CDs have become important materials for improving energy use efficiency, environmental management, health protection, and precision instrumentation technologies [32–35]. New carbon nanomaterials smaller than 10 nm in size can significantly improve the electron transfer efficiency and electrocatalytic activity of materials when carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are doped into solid phase carriers [36–39]. There are different approaches to CQD production [32,40]. The production of CQDs by the hydrothermal method has important features such as low cost, environmentally friendly and non-toxic production. The basic logic in hydrothermal production is the breakdown of organic structure into molecules at high temperatures and pressure [41–43].

In a study, a hydrothermal method is reported to increase the particle ratio of metal ions in the pores of the CQD support. It has been stated that a large number of functional groups (such as –COOH and –NH2) on the surface of CQDs used as supports can coordinate with most metals with empty d orbitals to form a relatively stable CQDs-metal ions composite material [39]. It is important in terms of production cost that CQDs prepared from different carbon sources are green and cheap sources. Caffeine, an active alkaloid component found in many natural products, is consumed in coffee, tea and beverages. Pure caffeine is a white odourless powder [44]. The size and morphological properties of the solvent and catalyst used during the preparation of the catalysts can be affected. Thus, the catalytic activities of catalysts prepared in different solvent environments can also be affected for the same application. To date, a limited number of studies have been reported on this subject. In the present study, caffeine CQD-supported Co catalysts were synthesized in methanol, water and ethanol solvents with different polarities and viscosities [45].

In this study, a new catalyst system was obtained by fixing Co particles prepared in different solvent environments on CQD support by hydrothermal method to produce H2 from KBH4 hydrolysis. UV–Vis and PL spectrophotometers were used to determine the formation of CQDs and the complexation between CQDs and metal ions. The properties of the synthesized catalyst X-ray powder diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), N2-physical adsorption and desorption, transmission electron microscopy (TEM), energy dispersive spectroscopy analysis (EDS), X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), inductively coupled plasma mass characterized by spectrometry (ICP-MS).
In this study, NaBH4 (Merck), KOH (Sigma-Aldrich), cobalt (II) chloride hexahydrate (Sigma-Aldrich), caffeine (Sigma-Aldrich), ethanol (Tekkim, Turkey) and methanol (Tekkim, Turkey) were used.
The crystalline and amorphous properties of these catalysts were determined in the range of 2θ:10°–80° with XRD (PANalytical Empyrean) with Cu-Ka radiation (λ = 1.5418 A).

The BET surface areas of these catalysts were determined at −196 °C by N2 isothermal adsorption (Quantachrome Nova 2200e). In addition, the pore properties of the catalysts were determined by the BJH method.
The surfactant groups of these catalysts were determined in the range of 650–4000 cm−1 with the FTIR spectrum (Nicolet 520).
The surface morphologies of the catalysts were analysed by TEM (Hitachi High Tech HT7700).
The surface components of the catalysts were investigated by energy dispersive spectroscopy analysis (EDS, ZEISS Sigma 300).
The Co content in the catalyst composite supported by inductively coupled plasma (ICP, Agilent 7800 model) was determined.

The basic properties of the catalysts were analysed by XPS (Specs-Flex X-Ray photoelectron spectrometer) in a high vacuum system with a base pressure of 2 × 10 −10 mbar using Al Ka radiation. XPS experiment parameters were selected as analyser phoibos, analyzer lens: midfield: 1.5 kv and source: XR 50 M (UXC1000), analyser lens: midfield: 1.5kv, analyser slit: 1:0.2 × 20\b: open, scan mode: steady analyser conduction, curves/scan: 1, values/curve: 1388, residence time: 0.4, excitation energy: 1486.71, binding energy: 1387, transition energy: 100, bias voltage: 0, detector voltage: 1580 and active working function: 4332. The materials were applied directly to the surface of a standard sample carrier and placed in the measuring chamber of the spectrometer. Gaussian Fit, Shirley background subtraction and straight-line shape were used in XPS analysis. Origin Pro 9.00 rendering software used for spectrum separation.
Absorption spectra of the carbon spot sample were obtained with a UV–Vis/fluorescence spectrophotometer (Perkin Elmer LS 55 Fluorescent).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Carbon quantum dot and catalyst synthesis
ce:section.ce:para: To obtain CQDs from caffeine by the hydrothermal method, a certain amount of caffeine and pure water is first left in a furnace. As the optimum parameters in the oven, the waiting time was 8 h and the temperature was 180 °C. The best hydrothermal production conditions were tried to be determined at different temperatures and residence times according to the photoluminescence effect under UV light (360 nm). After determining the ideal working range for hydrothermal production, CQD production was achieved. The produced CQD was passed through a 0.45-μm filter by pressure filtration to remove impurities. The filtered CQD was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 60 min. Support loading type was done by co-precipitation, impregnation and hydrothermal loading methods. These methods include impregnation loading of organic structures without any treatment, impregnation loading after fabrication of carbon quantum dot structures, and loading of CQD structures under hydrothermal conditions. The catalyst obtained by the precipitation method was labelled Co@caffeine, the catalyst obtained by the impregnation method was Co@CQD, and the catalyst obtained by the hydrothermal charging method was labelled Co@MOF-CQD (water). In co-precipitation, the uncharged state of the pure metal structure Co (0) was synthesized for comparison.

The reduction was carried out by dropping Co salt and 10 mmol of NaBH4 in 10 ml solvent (methanol, ethanol or methanol) at room conditions. It was observed that the cobalt species started to precipitate after 36–40 h at the end of the waiting period for the precipitation process to take place. Centrifugation was applied at 5000 rpm after reduction to determine the effect of residence time on catalyst activity. After reduction, it was determined that the cobalt structures were completely collapsed by centrifugation at 10,000 rpm for 45 min. The CQD-supported catalyst synthesized in ethanol medium was labelled Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol).

To catalyse the hydrolysis of KBH4 with CQD-supported Co catalysts, hydrolysis of KBH4 was carried out with a reactor system consisting of a circulating water bath, magnetic stirrer, jacketed tube and gas burette and connecting hoses. In the experimental phase, 30 mg of catalyst and a certain weight of KOH were added to a reaction flask. Then, this reaction flask was capped and placed in a water bath. A certain proportion of KBH4 was added to this reaction flask to initiate the reaction. The volume of H2 formed was measured in a scaled burette by the water displacement method. The effects of KBH4 concentration, KOH concentration and temperature were investigated in KBH4 hydrolysis applications. The performance of the catalyst was evaluated by the average hydrogen production rate (HGR), defined as ml min−1gcat −1. Repeatability experiments of the catalyst were performed six times in succession. After each experiment, 90% of the liquid fraction was removed by decantation in KBH4 hydrolysis and studied by adding new KBH4.

The calculation of HGR is given in Equation 2 given below.
(2)
r =
v
t .
m
c a t
where V is total hydrogen volume; r is the hydrogen production rate; mcat is the amount of the catalyst and t is the reaction time.

The UV–visible absorption spectra (a) and fluorescence spectra (b) of CQD (caffeine) and Co@CQD are shown in Fig. 1
. As can be seen from the UV–Vis spectrum, CQD (caffeine) and Co@CQD have significant absorption in the ultraviolet region (260–300 nm). Due to the π-π∗ conjugate transition of CC and the n-π∗ transition of C–O bonds, the maximum absorption peak is around 270 nm. However, the absorption peak density of Co@CQD is weaker compared to CQD. Photoluminescence (PL) spectra of CQD (caffeine) and Co@CQD samples synthesized at an excitation wavelength of 360 nm are shown in Fig. 2b. CQD (caffeine) emission ranging from 300–450 nm gives an emission peak at about 360 nm. The PL density appears to be significantly reduced, confirming the increased quantum excess of the Co@CQD (caffeine) sample synthesized by incorporating Co atoms into CQD (caffeine). The possible reason for this result may be due to the narrowing of the electron transitions of Co atoms attached to the CQD surface. This result could also mean that Co atoms are attached to the surface of the CQD.

As shown in Fig. 2, XRD analyses of Co (0), Co@caffeine, Co@CQD, Co@CQD-MOF(water) and Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) nanocomposites were performed. X-ray diffraction patterns for all samples showed the amorphous carbon structure. A sloping peak at approximately 46° can also be seen for all samples. The visible diffraction peak at 2θ = 46.6 can be assigned to the (200) plane of CoO (JCPDS No. 48–1719) [46]. This detection also indicates a higher degree of graphitization in the Co-incorporated sample. It has been widely reported that the transition metal can promote the graphitization of carbon materials, further increasing the electronic conductivity [47].

TEM analyses were performed to observe the micromorphology and structure of Co(0), Co@caffeine, Co@CQD, Co@CQD-MOF(water) and Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) nanocomposites. TEM analyses for Co (0) (Fig. 3 a) confirm aggregation of Co species. As can be seen in Fig. 3b–(e), a large number of Co and CoO nanoparticles are homogeneously dispersed in the matrix of caffeine carbon and carbon quantum dots. The CoO formation can be attributed to the oxidation of small Co nanoparticles during the exposure of samples to different solvent environments. It should be noted that nano-sized pores are present in the carbon matrix, as shown in the white regions of the TEM images. The TEM results show that some bright and dark areas are formed on the surface of the composites, representing the formation of the porous structure and Co particles. TEM images and size distribution histograms of Co (0), Co@caffeine, Co@CQD, Co@CQD-MOF(water) and Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) samples show average particle sizes of approximately 17.6 nm, 5.92 nm, 5.49 nm, 4.77 nm, and 5.95 nm, respectively. It can be attributed to the prevention of aggregation of particles by reducing the average particle size with both caffeine and carbon quantum dots materials. This result supports the formation of new particles with high catalytic activity by reducing the agglomeration of nanoparticles.

One of the important parameters that increase the performance of metal nanoparticles in catalytic applications is particle size [48]. As in NaBH4 catalytic hydrolysis, the catalyst exhibits better activity on the metal surface, with smaller metal particles having higher surface area and higher activity in KBH4 hydrolysis [49]. The particle size results obtained from the TEM analyses show that there is a significant difference between the Co catalysts used in this study. The importance of particle size for Co-catalysts supported on CQD may be related to the accessibility of the reactants to reach the active catalyst surface [30]. According to the Holbrook and Twist reaction mechanism, smaller metal particles are expected to have higher catalytic activity due to the larger available surface area/volume ratio. There are similar approaches and results in the literature [30,49,50].

EDX analysis for Co@CQD-MOF(water) (a) and Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) (b) nanocomposites is given in Fig. 4 . EDX analyses for both samples show that there are C, O, N and Co elements in the structure. These results show that Co metal has successfully adhered to the support material. The Co content for both samples was found to be 50% by weight. At the same time, the oxygen content for both samples was found to be about 30%.

The FTIR spectra of Co (0) (a), Co@caffeine (b), Co@CQD(c), Co@CQD-MOF (water) (d) and Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) (e) nanocomposites are shown in Fig. 5 . As can be seen, the FTIR spectra are similar to each other. However, especially for Co@CQD-MOF(water) and Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) nanocomposites, there is an increase in the peak intensities due to the increase of oxygen-containing functional groups around 1500 cm−1 and 1125 cm−1. The Brönsted and Lewis acid sites of the catalysts are important for catalytic applications. The peaks in the FT-IR spectrum, particularly in 1500 cm−1 and 1600 cm−1 ranges, correspond to the Brönsted and Lewis acid regions. For Co@CQD-MOF (water) (d) and Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) (e) nanocomposites, the intensity of the peak observed at 1502 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum is more pronounced compared to other catalysts. This peak at 1502 cm−1 can be interpreted as a combined movement of the Brønsted and Lewis sites. These results showed that Co@CQD-MOF (water) (d) and Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) (e) nanocomposites have important Lewis acid sites and can be used to catalyze efficient hydrogen production. The enrichment of Lewis acidic sites contributes to the increase in catalytic activity [51,52]. For Co@CQD-MOF (water) (d) and Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) (e) nanocomposites, there is an increase in the intensity of the peaks observed at approximately 754 cm−1 in the FTIR spectrum compared to other catalysts. This band at 754 cm−1 indicates that Co metal ions are successfully coordinated with the CQD support material [53,54].

Fig. 7 shows the XPS spectra of the CQD derived from caffeine, the Co@CQD-MOF (water) and Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) composites. It is seen that C 1s, N 1s and O 1s peaks are formed in the XPS graph of pure CQD obtained from caffeine under hydrothermal conditions and the binding energies of these peaks are 284, 398 and 529 eV, respectively. However, XPS spectra of Co@CQD-MOF (water) and Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) composites show that C and N peak intensities decrease in the structure and there are two peaks at 780 eV and 795 eV binding energies against Co 2p3/2 level. This result showed that the Co element exists in both elemental and oxidized states.

The C1s spectrum (Fig. 8 a) on the Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) composite gave three peaks on the carbon surface at 290.56, 285.63 and 281.96 eV, corresponding to C–N/CO, C–O and C–C, respectively [55]. The N 1s spectrum at 397.9, 399.9, 401 and 402.02 eV (Fig. 8b) showed the pyridinic-N, Co-Nx, pyrrolic-N and graphitic-N [56]. O 1s spectrum (Fig. 8c) showed the presence of oxides (Co__O), carboxyl groups and hydroxyl groups at 530.98 eV, 532.01eV and 533.03 eV, respectively. The presence of Co2+ signals (Fig. 8d) at 781.93 and 797.93 eV with the satellite peaks at 786.32, as well as 804.02 eV, may source from the oxidized surface of the material [57]. Two peaks situated at 793.63 eV are assigned to metallic Co0 [58]. The carbon, nitrogen, oxygen and cobalt contents for this Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) sample with XPS were found to be 20.83%, 1.49%, 48.15%, and 21.41%, by weight, respectively. These results differ from those found with EDS. Therefore, the cobalt content in the Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) sample was examined by ICP-MS and 54.62% by weight of cobalt was found. This is because cobalt nanoparticles embedded in the carbon-layered CQD support are probably difficult to detect by XPS, as only the valence state of the surface cobalt nanoparticles can be detected with XPS. It is also possible that the metallic cobalt nanoparticles on the catalyst surface of the cobalt nanoparticles can be oxidized to cobalt oxides, and that the metallic cobalt nanoparticles on the surface of the supported catalyst have a strong electronic interaction with carbon, oxygen or nitrogen atoms [59].

The H2 production volumes obtained from KBH4 hydrolysis with Co (0), Co@caffeine, Co@CQD (caffeine) and Co@MOF-CQD (water) catalysts are shown in Fig. 9 . HGR values of 1839, 7860, 9333, and 10,490 ml min−1gcat −1 were found from Co (0), Co@caffeine, Co@CQD (caffeine), Co@MOF-CQD (water) catalysts for KBH4 hydrolysis, respectively. As can be understood, there is a significant increase in HGR values with both caffeine and CQD support materials obtained from caffeine used for Co particles. The TEM analysis results showed that the average particle sizes with these support materials were quite low compared to the Co (0) catalyst under different production conditions. It has been stated that the activity in catalytic reactions increases as the size of the metal particles decreases [60,61]. The possible reason for this result can be explained by the fact that the support material shows a more effective catalytic performance by preventing metal aggregation. At the same time, the oxidation of carbon nanodot-supported cobalt particles may have a significant effect on the catalytic activity, in conjunction with the increase of oxygen groups on the catalyst surface, as indicated by XRD, EDS, XPS and FTIR analyses. These results are consistent with the literature [60,62].

H2 production from KBH4 with supported Co catalyst prepared in methanol, ethanol and water solvent media was investigated. The catalysts prepared in different solvent environments have significant effects on H2 production from KBH4 hydrolysis. The HGR values obtained with catalysts synthesized in water, methanol and ethanol were 6200, 8400 and 9200 ml min−1gcat −1, respectively. As can be seen from the HGR values, the catalysts synthesized in methanol and ethanol solvents showed a higher catalytic performance compared to pure water. The highest catalytic performance was achieved with the catalyst prepared in ethanol. The results show that solvents can significantly affect the morphology of the catalyst. These results imply that the cobalt particles favour homogeneous dispersion on the CQD support produced from the caffeine in the ethanol solvent. Thus, the aggregation of cobalt particles was prevented by using ethanol as a solvent to prepare a catalyst [63]. Solvents can greatly affect the size and morphology of catalysts synthesized in solution. Carbon dot-supported Co catalyst prepared in ethanol solvent exhibits faster H2 conversion, indicating that ethanol is a modification solvent. The degree of reduction of cobalt particles can be increased by ethoxy ions from ethanol on the surface of the support material [64]. Thus, ethanol can promote the fixation of cobalt atoms on the support surface and increase the cobalt dispersion. The polarities of the solvents may affect the activity in catalytic applications. The reduced polarity of the solvent can cause an increase in the interaction between the cobalt hydrate and the CQD surface. There are similar approaches in the literature [65]. The improved distribution of the supported metal will contribute to creating a more active site that is advantageous for any structure-sensitive reaction. Also, because cobalt nitrate has a greater solubility in water than in ethanol, the concentration of cobalt ions in isolated water is higher than in ethanol, resulting in a larger cobalt particle size in water [65]. TEM analyses support this conclusion.

Effects of initial KBH4 concentrations on the H2 generation by Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) catalyst at 30 °C were investigated. When the KBH4 concentration increases from 1% to 4% by weight, the H2 production rate increases from 14,087 ml min−1g−1 to 17,081 ml min−1g−1. However, the H2 production rate decreases from 17,081 ml min−1g−1 to 16,970 ml min−1g−1 by increasing the KBH4 concentration from 4% to 8% by weight. Due to the formation of a more hydrated by-product as a result of the reaction at a higher KBH4 concentration, the mass transport to the catalyst surface will be prevented and the reaction rate will decrease, with the viscosity of the solution increasing significantly [66]. In addition, it has been reported in some literature that the H2 production rate decreases with the borohydride concentration [67]. At the same time, TOF values obtained in the presence of 1%, 2%, 4% and 8% KBH4 were found to be 2243, 2615, 2765, and 2645 h−1, respectively.

H2 production volumes by a 2% wt% KBH4 and different wt% KOH (1%, 2.5%, 5%, 7.5%) solution at 30 °C using Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) catalyst were obtained. By increasing the KOH concentration from 1% to 2.5% by weight, the H2 production rate increases significantly from 11,535 ml min−1g−1 to 17,081 ml min−1g−1. However, the H2 production rate drops from 17,081 ml min−1g−1 to 10,722 ml min−1g−1 as the KOH concentration increases from 2.5% to 7.5% by weight. Alkaline is added to the borohydride solution to prevent a self-hydrolysis reaction. However, researchers have reported different results for the effect of alkali concentration on H2 generation behaviours [68]. By increasing the electron density of metallic nanoparticles at low NaOH concentration, the coordination of hydroxide ions to the catalyst surface can facilitate O–H cracking in water [69]. However, at high NaOH concentrations, large amounts of OH− ions can occupy the surfactant sites of the catalysts, blocking the contact between the catalyst surface and the BH4 − reactant [70]. Thus, the catalytic activity is negatively affected at high NaOH concentrations. These considerations for NaOH can be considered in KOH.

Fig. 10 shows the effect of temperature (30, 40, 50 and 60 °C) on H2 production by hydrolysis reaction of KBH4 using Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) catalyst. As can be understood, there is an increase in the volume of hydrogen production from the KBH4 hydrolysis reaction with an increase in temperature. At 30 °C, the H2 production reaction was completed with Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) catalyst in approximately 4 min. At the same time, at temperatures of 40, 50 and 60 °C, this hydrolysis reaction was completed in 2, 1.25 and 0.83 min, respectively. The H2 production rates obtained at 30, 40, 50 and 60 °C were found to be 17,081, 38,405, 63,733 and 115,938 ml min−1g−1, respectively.

The activation energy of H2 production from the hydrolysis reaction with Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) catalyst with KBH4 can be estimated by the Arrhenius equation given in equation 2.
(3)
k = A · exp
( − Ea / RT )
here, k is the hydrogen production rate, A is the exponential factor, Ea is the activation energy (kJ mol−1), R is the ideal gas constant (8.314 J mol−1 K−1), and T is the temperature of the solution (K). From the Arrhenius equation, the activation energy for the hydrolysis reaction in the presence of Co@CQD-MOF (ethanol) catalyst was found to be 47.5 kJ mol−1. This activation value is compared with the catalyst systems used for KBH4 hydrolysis in Table 2 [19,68,71–77]. Table 2 presents both heterogeneous and homogeneous catalysts for H2 production from KBH4 hydrolysis. In particular, the catalyst used in this study showed significant performance when compared to the catalyst systems containing Co. From Tables 2, it can be stated that the catalytic performance of the Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) catalyst is ideal compared to other catalyst systems.

The reusability of Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) for the catalytic hydrolysis of KBH4 for H2 production was evaluated by six consecutive experiments and the resulting H2 production volumes are given in Fig. 11 . The 100% H2 conversion was achieved in all experiments. The H2 production rate for catalytic activity was 17,081 ml min−1gcat −1 in the first cycle, while this value drops to 15,190 ml min−1 gcat −1 after the second cycle. This decrease in the H2 production rate continues in successive cycles. In the sixth cycle, the H2 production rate was determined as 8767 ml min−1 gcat −1. The decrease in the rate of H2 production after reusability can be explained by the by-product (KBO2) deposited on the surface of the catalyst as a result of the reaction, blocking the active sites. And inhibits the hydrolysis of KBH4. Thus, the catalytic activity is adversely affected.

From the TEM analyses, the average particle sizes of the carbon dot-supported Co particles obtained from caffeine were quite small compared to the Co (0) sample. Such nanolayers can stabilize the cobalt atoms, prevent the cobalt atoms from clumping together, and have a more homogeneous distribution. This structure has a positive effect on H2 production by allowing more interaction between the reactants and the catalyst. In addition, using ethanol or methanol instead of water by hydrothermal treatment during the preparation of supported Co catalysts exhibited faster H2 conversion. Considering the acidity of aliphatic alcohols in their aqueous solutions, all other aliphatic alcohols except methanol are less acidic than water. The hydroxyl proton of alcohols with very weak Brønsted acids can be considered the most electrophilic region. Since water is more polar than ethanol and methanol, stronger O–H bonds induce the adsorption of Co metal species, resulting in a larger particle size. The acidity of ethanol is less than that of water, due to the electron repulsion of the ethoxy species formed by the dissolution of ethanol. Oxygen atoms in ethanol molecules coordinate Co species. However, aqueous solutions of ethanol are somewhat basic, with oxygen atoms in ethanol having lone electron pairs that can accept protons. In such an environment, faster absorption of Lewis bases can occur. Thus, by increasing the electron density of active metal atoms in a solution with such ions, electron-enriched metal active sites and negatively charged electrons needed by the hydrogen atom can positively affect the KBH4 hydrolysis reaction kinetics. In the literature, possible reactions in the preparation of metal-containing catalysts in water, methanol and ethanol solvents are given [45,78,79]. The same way of thinking can be considered for the Co metal-based catalyst on the caffeine carbon point support used in this study.

The proposed mechanism for metal (Co)-catalysed hydrolysis of KBH4 can be illustrated in the reaction steps given below [81–83].

Catalytic hydrolysis of KBH4 by the Langmuir-Hinshelwood type mechanism may be considered. BH4 − formed after the ionization of KBH4 and water molecules in the reaction medium is adsorbed on the surface of the supported Co catalyst. Thus, a complex structure is formed between the supported Co catalyst, BH4 −, and H2O. Next, the hydrogen atom from BH4 − is transferred to the adjacent catalyst. By adsorbing KBH4 molecules by Co metal atoms, it can activate B–H bonds and reduce the activation energy [84]. Due to the weak binding energy of Co metal atoms to H atoms, H atoms can be rapidly desorbed from the catalyst surface [85]. Next, the hydrogen atom takes an electron from the catalyst and leaves the catalyst to react with a water molecule in the hydridic form to produce H2 and OH−. Then, this hydroxide anion, which reacts with boron in BH3, forms BH3(OH)-. This cycle continues until B(OH)4 − is formed. XPS, FTIR and EDX analyses showed abundant oxygen atoms on the caffeine carbon dot-supported Co catalyst. O atoms on this catalyst surface can promote active site formation on the Co surface through hydrogen bonds with H2O molecules. At the same time, OH− ions in solution can bind to positively charged Co species through strong electrostatic interaction, accumulating abundant H+ atoms and OH− radicals. This formation will contribute to the production of H2 molecules. The production scheme of Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol) catalyst and H2 production from KBH4 hydrolysis are shown in Fig. 12 .

Herein, the efficiency of the Co catalyst synthesized with caffeine-based carbon dot support using ethanol was evaluated for efficient H2 production from the hydrolysis of KBH4. The chemical compositions and morphology structures of this caffeine carbon dot-supported Co catalysts were carefully characterized by XRD, TEM, BET, EDS, FTIR, XPS, and ICP/OES analyses. XPS, FTIR and EDX analyses showed abundant oxygen atoms on the caffeine carbon dot-supported Co catalyst. O atoms on this catalyst surface can promote active site formation on the Co surface through hydrogen bonds with H2O molecules. The average particle size value of the carbon dots obtained from caffeine is quite low compared to the Co (0) catalyst. It can be attributed to the prevention of aggregation of particles by reducing the average particle size with both caffeine and carbon quantum dots materials. H2 production from KBH4 with supported Co catalyst prepared in methanol, ethanol and water solvent media was investigated. The catalysts prepared in methanol and ethanol solvent environments by hydrothermal treatment compared to pure water have significant effects on H2 production from KBH4 hydrolysis. The HGR values obtained with catalysts synthesized in water, methanol and ethanol were 6200, 8400 and 9200 ml min−1gcat −1, respectively. The reusability of Co@CQD-MOF(ethanol) for the catalytic hydrolysis of KBH4 for H2 production was evaluated by six consecutive experiments and the 100% H2 conversion was achieved in all experiments. The H2 production rate obtained by Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol) at 30 °C was found to be 17,081 ml min−1g−1. At the same time, the TOF value obtained with 4% KBH4 concentrations for the Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol) was found as 2765 h−1. The Ea for the Co@MOF-CQD (ethanol) catalyst was 47.5 kJmol-1.
